summary: Jeff Atwood reflects on the rise and fall of WordPerfect through the lens of the book 'Almost Perfect' by W. E. Pete Peterson. WordPerfect dominated word processing from 1985-1992 but failed to transition from DOS to Windows, institutionalizing a character-mode software culture that proved fatal. The company was notable for being run by programmers who made product decisions, cultivating excellence and community. Atwood draws parallels to other forgotten software pioneers like Electric Pencil, noting how quickly dominant products can become footnotes in computing history.
key_points: WordPerfect's failure stemmed from not adapting to Windows and institutionalizing DOS-era assumptions
The company was unusually programmer-led, with developers making product decisions rather than marketing
Fanatical dedication to cross-platform parity, even for platforms that made no business sense, contributed to their downfall
Software dominance can evaporate remarkably quickly—Electric Pencil went from 250,000 users to forgotten in just 3 years
Success came from dedication to excellent products and cultivating vibrant internal and external communities
money_quotes: You can dominate the world for years, only to be relegated to little more than a dimly remembered footnote in computing history a decade later.
They didn't just bet on the wrong horse, they institutionalized a software culture that lived and died on character mode assumptions.
It was somewhat unusual for a software company to let the programmers decide the future of its products.
They clawed their way to success over so many formidable opponents by simply dedicating themselves to creating an excellent product.
At times this put us in the position of developing solutions before we identified problems.
Like WordPerfect itself, he doesn't go out with a bang, but a whimper.
key_insight: Even total market dominance offers no protection when a company institutionalizes assumptions about technology that become obsolete—adaptability matters more than current success.
